prompt: A developer asks for an order-of-cost estimate at RIBA Stage 1 for a 12,000 m² office in Manchester. Which approach best meets NRM1 and Level 3 design economics practice?

options.text: Prepare an order-of-cost estimate using appropriate functional units, benchmark rates, and stated assumptions with documented exclusions
Issue a fixed lump sum guaranteed to tender without any elemental breakdown
Measure every item to SMM7 rules from outline drawings only
Use only the contractor's last project rate with no location or inflation adjustment

explanation: NRM1 order-of-cost estimates at early stages use functional units and benchmarks with transparent assumptions — a core Level 3 cost planning skill.

distractorExplanations.b: Stage 1 estimates are indicative and cannot guarantee tender outcomes without design detail.
distractorExplanations.c: Detailed measurement is premature at Stage 1; order-of-cost methods apply.
distractorExplanations.d: Benchmarks must reflect project-specific factors and current market conditions.

prompt: At RIBA Stage 2, your elemental cost plan shows superstructure at £18.5m against a £17m budget. The architect proposes value engineering. What is your first professional action?

options.text: Delete the superstructure element to balance the total automatically
Quantify cost drivers, present options with cost/carbon/function trade-offs, and update the cost plan with agreed adjustments
Wait until tender to address the overspend
Instruct the architect to reduce quality without cost analysis

explanation: Level 3 design economics involves diagnosing overspends, facilitating VE, and revising elemental cost plans with documented rationale.

distractorExplanations.a: Cost plans must reflect design reality, not arbitrary element removal.
distractorExplanations.c: Cost planning requires proactive advice before procurement.
distractorExplanations.d: Value engineering needs structured option appraisal, not unilateral quality cuts.

prompt: A PQS is appointed on a design-and-build hospital. The employer's agent requests a cost plan at RIBA Stage 3 aligned to NRM1. Which deliverable is most appropriate?

options.text: A contractor's priced BOQ only with no cost plan structure
A single-line total with no elemental split
An elemental cost plan with sub-element breakdown, quantified where possible, tied to the current design and updated assumptions
A post-contract final account format

explanation: RICS QS cost planning at Stage 3 delivers NRM-aligned elemental cost plans supporting employer budget control.

distractorExplanations.a: Employer-side cost planning follows NRM elemental structure, not trade pricing alone.
distractorExplanations.b: Stage 3 cost plans require elemental transparency for design decisions.
distractorExplanations.d: Final accounts apply after construction; Stage 3 needs forward-looking cost plans.

prompt: Your client's gateway review requires demonstration that the £45m GIA budget is still viable at RIBA Stage 4. Which evidence best supports Level 3 advice?

options.text: Verbal assurance that the market is favourable
A list of provisional sums with no reconciliation to design
Only the architect's specification without cost translation
Updated elemental cost plan with measured quantities where available, reconciled to previous stages and tender strategy

explanation: Stage 4 cost plans integrate measurement progress, design changes, and procurement strategy to confirm budget viability.

distractorExplanations.a: Gateway decisions require documented cost evidence, not unsupported opinion.
distractorExplanations.b: Provisional sums alone do not demonstrate overall budget viability.
distractorExplanations.c: Cost planners must translate design information into cost advice.

prompt: A contractor commercial manager must price a two-stage D&B frame agreement. Stage 1 target cost is £28m. Which commercial management action is most sound?

options.text: Build up target cost from supply chain input, risk register, and preliminaries with agreed pain/gain mechanisms
Add 30% margin to the employer's budget without analysis
Exclude preliminaries because they are fixed on every job
Ignore second-stage subcontract competition

explanation: Commercial management at Level 3 involves transparent target-cost build-ups and risk-aligned commercial structures.

distractorExplanations.b: Commercial pricing requires substantiated build-ups, not arbitrary margins.
distractorExplanations.c: Preliminaries vary by programme, site, and methodology and must be priced.
distractorExplanations.d: Two-stage procurement relies on competitive second-stage pricing.

prompt: An employer asks whether to procure a £6m fit-out as traditional or design-and-build. You are providing design economics advice. What should you compare?

options.text: Only the lowest single tender received last year on a different building
Whole-life implications, cost certainty, programme, design risk allocation, and tender price dispersion for each route
Architect preference without cost or risk comparison
Assume D&B is always cheapest regardless of scope definition

explanation: Design economics includes procurement route appraisal balancing cost, risk, programme, and quality.

distractorExplanations.a: Route selection needs project-specific analysis, not unrelated benchmarks.
distractorExplanations.c: Procurement advice must be evidence-based across relevant criteria.
distractorExplanations.d: Cost outcomes depend on scope, market, and risk transfer.

prompt: BCIS location and tender price indices have moved +4.2% since your last cost plan. The project is 14 months from anticipated tender. What adjustment is professionally appropriate?

options.text: Ignore inflation because the budget was approved
Increase every element by 15% without documentation
Apply an evidence-based inflation allowance to relevant elements with stated basis and review at next stage
Defer all inflation to the contractor at tender

explanation: Cost planners use BCIS or equivalent indices to project tender-time costs with documented assumptions.

distractorExplanations.a: Approved budgets still require inflation transparency in forward estimates.
distractorExplanations.b: Inflation adjustments should be justified and element-specific where possible.
distractorExplanations.d: Employer cost plans should reflect anticipated market movement.

prompt: A university client wants lifecycle cost advice for cladding options: aluminium rainscreen vs brick slip. Which analysis best reflects Level 3 design economics?

options.text: Select the lowest capital cost option only
Exclude maintenance because it is an FM issue
Use manufacturer marketing data without independent verification
Compare capital cost, maintenance, replacement cycles, energy performance, and whole-life cost over an agreed period

explanation: Level 3 design economics integrates capital and operational costs for informed option selection.

distractorExplanations.a: Design economics considers whole-life performance, not capital cost alone.
distractorExplanations.b: Whole-life costing is integral to cladding option appraisal.
distractorExplanations.c: Advice should be based on verifiable technical and cost data.

prompt: Your elemental cost plan includes a 10% design contingency at RIBA Stage 3. The project manager asks why it remains when design is '80% complete'. How do you respond?

options.text: Explain contingency covers unresolved design, scope, and market risk until tender — reducing it only with evidenced design maturity
Remove all contingency to show you are under budget
Transfer contingency to contractor profit
Hide contingency inside every unit rate without disclosure

explanation: Design contingencies reflect residual uncertainty and should be managed transparently through stages.

distractorExplanations.b: Premature contingency removal misstates risk and misleads the client.
distractorExplanations.c: Contingency is a risk allowance, not a profit transfer mechanism.
distractorExplanations.d: Cost plans should transparently show risk allowances.

prompt: A commercial director on a main contractor framework asks you to review three tender returns for a £12m school extension. Returns range £11.4m–£13.1m. What is your best next step?

options.text: Recommend the lowest price immediately to win work
Analyse scope compliance, qualifications, preliminaries, risk, programme, and resource assumptions before recommendation
Average the three prices and award to the middle bidder
Reject all tenders and re-tender without analysis

explanation: Commercial management includes rigorous tender return analysis beyond headline price.

distractorExplanations.a: Lowest price may reflect exclusions, risk mispricing, or insolvency risk.
distractorExplanations.c: Tender analysis requires qualitative and quantitative review, not averaging.
distractorExplanations.d: Structured analysis should precede any re-tender decision.

prompt: At feasibility, a client provides 2,500 m² net office area and asks for budget advice in central London. Which functional unit approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Cost per employee headcount only with no area basis
Use industrial warehouse benchmarks without adjustment
Cost per m² GIA or NIA using relevant BCIS benchmarks with adjustments for specification, basement, and site constraints
Apply residential £/m² rates to office accommodation

explanation: Feasibility budgeting uses appropriate functional units with transparent benchmark adjustments.

distractorExplanations.a: Early office estimates typically use area-based functional units with stated basis.
distractorExplanations.b: Building type and location strongly affect benchmark selection.
distractorExplanations.d: Functional units must match building use and specification level.

prompt: A design change adds a rooftop plant enclosure. The architect asks if it is 'free' because the plant was already budgeted. What is the correct cost planning view?

options.text: Treat all design changes as zero cost if within contingency
Ignore structural implications of added load
Defer costing until the final account
Assess incremental cost of enclosure, structural support, access, and services diversions beyond the base plant allowance

explanation: Design economics requires incremental cost assessment of changes against baseline allowances.

distractorExplanations.a: Changes should be valued incrementally; contingency is not automatic cover.
distractorExplanations.b: Cost impact includes associated structural and MEP modifications.
distractorExplanations.c: Cost planning provides timely change cost advice during design.

prompt: You are preparing a pre-tender estimate at RIBA Stage 4 using approximate quantities. Which document set is essential?

options.text: Coordinated drawings, specifications, provisional quantities, and defined measurement rules with exclusions listed
Outline planning sketches from Stage 1 only
Contractor's priced BOQ from another project
Verbal scope from the client without written record

explanation: Pre-tender estimates at Stage 4 rely on coordinated design and stated measurement basis.

distractorExplanations.b: Pre-tender estimates need current coordinated design information.
distractorExplanations.c: Estimates must reflect this project's design and scope.
distractorExplanations.d: Professional cost advice requires documented scope and assumptions.

prompt: A client's cost limit for a refurbishment is £8m. Your order-of-cost estimate is £9.3m at Stage 2. What is the most professional recommendation?

options.text: Proceed silently and hope tender is lower
Present options to align scope, specification, or phasing to the budget with quantified savings and residual risk
Tell the client the budget is unrealistic without offering solutions
Reduce the estimate by removing VAT from the total

explanation: When estimates exceed budget, QS advice includes scoped options to close the gap.

distractorExplanations.a: Cost planners must flag budget gaps with actionable options.
distractorExplanations.c: Level 3 advice includes optioneering, not only problem identification.
distractorExplanations.d: Estimates must correctly treat tax and statutory costs per brief.

prompt: On a contractor's scheme, you must set a selling price for a negotiated D&B office. Direct costs are £22m, risk £1.2m, overheads 6%, profit target 3%. What commercial principle applies?

options.text: Hide risk inside overheads to simplify the bid
Quote £22m only because that is the direct cost
Present a transparent build-up separating cost, risk, overheads, and profit with documented assumptions for negotiation
Match a competitor's price without internal cost review

explanation: Contractor commercial management uses structured price build-ups for governance and negotiation.

distractorExplanations.a: Commercial transparency supports negotiation and internal governance.
distractorExplanations.b: Selling price must reflect risk, overheads, and profit policy.
distractorExplanations.d: Commercial management requires cost-led pricing decisions.

prompt: An employer's cost report shows a 6% forecast overrun against approved budget at Stage 4. Under design economics practice, what should the report include?

options.text: Only a green RAG status to avoid alarming the client
Delete variances from previous reports
Blame the contractor before appointment
Cause analysis, forecast final cost, mitigations, contingency status, and decisions required from the client

explanation: Employer cost reports at Level 3 enable informed decisions with clear variance analysis.

distractorExplanations.a: Cost reporting must be honest and decision-focused.
distractorExplanations.b: Reports should maintain continuity and trend analysis.
distractorExplanations.c: Pre-contract reports focus on design, scope, and procurement factors.

prompt: A mixed-use scheme needs separate cost plans for residential and commercial elements for funding purposes. How should you structure this?

options.text: Split elemental cost plans by use with shared infrastructure apportioned on an agreed basis
Produce one blended cost plan with no use split
Allocate all basement costs to residential only
Exclude common parts entirely from the cost plan

explanation: Multi-use schemes require transparent cost allocation between uses and shared elements.

distractorExplanations.b: Funding and appraisal often require use-specific cost allocation.
distractorExplanations.c: Shared elements need agreed apportionment methodology.
distractorExplanations.d: Common parts are real costs and must be included.

prompt: A project uses target cost with 60:40 pain/gain share. Final cost is £500k below target. How should commercial management treat the gain?

options.text: Retain all savings as additional contractor profit by default
Apply the agreed share mechanism, document calculation, and adjust final payment per contract terms
Ignore the mechanism because the client is public sector
Transfer gain to the next project without client agreement

explanation: Target-cost commercial management includes correct pain/gain administration.

distractorExplanations.a: Pain/gain shares are contractual and must be applied as agreed.
distractorExplanations.c: Public sector projects still follow agreed commercial mechanisms.
distractorExplanations.d: Gain share calculations are project-specific contractual matters.

prompt: Your client asks for carbon and cost integrated advice at Stage 3 for structural frame options. What aligns with contemporary QS design economics?

options.text: Ignore carbon because it is not a cost element
Use only operational energy data for structural choice
Compare embodied carbon and capital cost for steel, concrete, and timber options with whole-life implications stated
Select the lightest frame without engineering or cost input

explanation: Design economics now commonly integrates cost and embodied carbon in option appraisals.

distractorExplanations.a: Modern cost planning increasingly integrates carbon with cost optioneering.
distractorExplanations.b: Structural optioneering focuses on embodied carbon and capital cost.
distractorExplanations.d: Options need coordinated structural, cost, and sustainability advice.

prompt: A cost plan at Stage 3 shows MEP services at 38% of construction cost versus a 32% benchmark. What is your professional response?

options.text: Force MEP to 32% by arbitrary rate reduction
Ignore variance because benchmarks are never useful
Delete MEP sub-elements to hide the variance
Investigate drivers — plant complexity, sustainability targets, riser strategy — and report findings with normalisation basis

explanation: Benchmark variance analysis is a key Level 3 cost planning diagnostic skill.

distractorExplanations.a: Benchmark variances need investigation, not arbitrary adjustment.
distractorExplanations.b: Benchmarks are diagnostic tools requiring professional interpretation.
distractorExplanations.c: Cost plans must remain complete and transparent.

prompt: An employer wants 'cost certainty' before committing to D&B tender. At Stage 4, what realistically can you advise?

options.text: Tender-stage cost plan with defined scope, quantified provisional items, and stated residual risks affecting certainty
Guarantee zero variation after contract award
Promise the lowest tender will equal the cost plan exactly
Certify the contractor's design is complete at Stage 4 employer design

explanation: Cost certainty advice is qualified by scope definition, procurement route, and residual risks.

distractorExplanations.b: Residual design and site risks may still produce variations.
distractorExplanations.c: Tender outcomes depend on market pricing and scope compliance.
distractorExplanations.d: Certainty advice must reflect actual scope definition and procurement route.

prompt: A commercial manager reviews supply chain quotations for a £2m cladding package. One subbie is 20% below others. What should you check first?

options.text: Award immediately to improve margin
Scope compliance, exclusions, programme, insurances, technical compliance, and financial standing
Assume all quotes are interchangeable
Select the highest quote to avoid failure

explanation: Commercial supply-chain review examines price alongside scope, risk, and capability.

distractorExplanations.a: Abnormally low prices may indicate risk, error, or exclusion.
distractorExplanations.c: Quotations require scope and qualification comparison.
distractorExplanations.d: Price alone does not determine suitability; balanced analysis is required.

prompt: RICS Level 3 design economics requires you to explain cost plan evolution to APC assessors. Which narrative is strongest?

options.text: That you only produced one spreadsheet at the end
That the architect set all costs without QS input
How estimates progressed from order-of-cost to pre-tender with key design decisions, VE, and market feedback
That cost plans are confidential and cannot be explained

explanation: APC Level 3 design economics is demonstrated through staged cost plan evolution and advice.

distractorExplanations.a: Level 3 requires demonstrated evolution and advice through stages.
distractorExplanations.b: Candidates must show personal cost planning leadership.
distractorExplanations.d: APC requires articulate explanation of your cost planning decisions.

prompt: A phased office development has Phase 1 funded now and Phase 2 in three years. How should cost planning treat Phase 2?

options.text: Combine both phases into one current-year total without escalation
Exclude Phase 2 entirely from client advice
Assume Phase 2 costs equal Phase 1 with no review
Separate cost plan with escalation assumptions, enabling works allowances, and interface costs between phases

explanation: Phased developments need stage-specific cost plans and interface cost allowances.

distractorExplanations.a: Phasing requires time-adjusted and interface-aware costing.
distractorExplanations.b: Masterplan cost advice should address all committed phases.
distractorExplanations.c: Future phases need updated assumptions and market outlook.

prompt: Your employer client requests inclusion of FF&E and loose equipment in the construction cost plan for a hotel. What is correct practice?

options.text: Include as separate cost groups with clear exclusion from building contract scope where appropriate
Merge FF&E into concrete rates
Exclude all FF&E as never part of QS remit
Double-count FF&E in both preliminaries and finishes

explanation: Hotel and fit-out cost planning separates building works from FF&E with clear scope boundaries.

distractorExplanations.b: FF&E should be identifiable and scope-separated from building works.
distractorExplanations.c: Total project cost advice often includes client-defined FF&E allowances.
distractorExplanations.d: Cost plans must avoid duplication and show clear scope.

prompt: A design team proposes reducing floor-to-floor height to save cost. What cost planning analysis is needed?

options.text: Approve immediately because any reduction saves money
Quantify savings in cladding, structure, and MEP against impacts on services capacity, acoustics, and net area
Reject without analysis to save time
Consider only door hardware savings

explanation: VE on floor-to-floor height requires cross-discipline cost and performance trade-off analysis.

distractorExplanations.a: Design changes need net cost and performance impact analysis.
distractorExplanations.c: Cost planners should evaluate VE proposals objectively.
distractorExplanations.d: Floor-to-floor changes affect multiple elements and performance criteria.

prompt: At tender analysis for an employer, the lowest compliant bid is £410k below cost plan. What is the QS recommendation process?

options.text: Automatic award to lowest without review
Reject low bid as always non-compliant
Review scope gaps, rates, programme, and qualifications; reconcile to cost plan; advise on award or clarification
Increase cost plan to match tender without scrutiny

explanation: Post-tender employer advice reconciles tenders to cost plans with compliance checks.

distractorExplanations.a: Tender analysis must confirm compliance and sustainability of price.
distractorExplanations.b: Low bids may be valid; analysis determines compliance.
distractorExplanations.d: Cost plan and tender reconciliation requires critical review.

prompt: A contractor commercial team must forecast preliminaries for an 18-month city centre rebuild. Which factor is most significant?

options.text: Only head office rent
Uniform £/m² rate from a rural warehouse project
Exclude welfare facilities to reduce cost
Site logistics, hoarding, craneage, working hours restrictions, and phased possession

explanation: Urban refurbishment preliminaries are heavily influenced by logistics and possession phasing.

distractorExplanations.a: Project preliminaries are driven by site-specific logistics and constraints.
distractorExplanations.b: Preliminaries must reflect urban constraints and programme.
distractorExplanations.c: Statutory site welfare is mandatory and must be included.

prompt: An options appraisal compares new build vs retention of a 1960s frame. Which cost planning input is essential?

options.text: Investigation allowances, remediation risk, temporary works, programme differential, and operational disruption costs
New build cost only without retention investigation
Assume retention is always cheaper
Ignore temporary works for phased decanting

explanation: New build vs retention appraisals need full lifecycle and risk-adjusted cost comparison.

distractorExplanations.b: Retention options need specific investigation and risk allowances.
distractorExplanations.c: Retention can carry hidden remediation and programme costs.
distractorExplanations.d: Decanting and temporary works are major cost drivers in retention schemes.

prompt: A client's funders require quarterly cost reports during design. What minimum content meets Level 3 standards?

options.text: A one-line email stating 'on budget'
Budget status, design development impact, risk/contingency, forecast final cost, and actions for approval
Only architect progress photos
Post-construction cash flow only

explanation: Funder-grade cost reporting during design tracks budget, risk, and required decisions.

distractorExplanations.a: Funder reporting requires structured cost intelligence.
distractorExplanations.c: Cost reports must address financial status and forecast.
distractorExplanations.d: Design-stage reporting focuses on cost plan and budget trajectory.

prompt: You are commercial manager on a loss-making project at 70% complete. Forecast final cost exceeds sell value. What is the appropriate action?

options.text: Hide the forecast until project completion
Stop all cost recording to avoid evidence
Reforecast, identify recovery options, escalate commercially, and document margin mitigation with site and head office
Automatically claim variations without merit to recover margin

explanation: Commercial management includes honest forecasting and structured recovery on distressed jobs.

distractorExplanations.a: Commercial management requires timely transparent forecasting.
distractorExplanations.b: Accurate cost control is essential even on distressed projects.
distractorExplanations.d: Claims must be contractually and factually substantiated.

prompt: A cost plan element for external works is based on a single vendor quote for paving. What weakness should you flag?

options.text: No weakness if the vendor is well known
External works need no cost plan element
Replace external works with a provisional sum always
Single-source pricing lacks competitive market test; recommend benchmark or tender comparison

explanation: Cost planning should challenge single-source pricing with market evidence.

distractorExplanations.a: Cost plans should reflect market testing where possible.
distractorExplanations.b: External works are a standard NRM element requiring allowance.
distractorExplanations.c: Provisional sums are for undefined scope, not lazy pricing.

prompt: An employer's agent asks you to align the cost plan to JCT Design & Build employer's requirements. What linkage is key?

options.text: Cost plan scope and exclusions must mirror ERs, drawings, and stated provisional items
Cost plan can ignore ER exclusions
Only the contract sum matters after award
Use NEC wording in a JCT cost plan without adjustment

explanation: Pre-tender cost plans must align with contract documentation scope.

distractorExplanations.b: Misalignment creates contract and budget risk.
distractorExplanations.c: Pre-contract cost plans must align to tender documentation.
distractorExplanations.d: Documentation and cost plan must match the chosen contract form.

prompt: A developer requests sensitivity analysis on steel price volatility for a 30-storey residential tower. What do you provide?

options.text: A generic note that materials fluctuate
Elemental sensitivity on steel package with percentage and absolute impact on total cost and funding headroom
Ignore steel because it is subcontracted
Fix steel price for three years without market basis

explanation: Design-stage sensitivity analysis quantifies material price risk on total project cost.

distractorExplanations.a: Sensitivity analysis should quantify specific material impacts.
distractorExplanations.c: Main material risks affect overall project cost and funding.
distractorExplanations.d: Sensitivity should reflect realistic market volatility scenarios.

prompt: Commercial management requires you to set a bid strategy for a competitive two-envelope tender. What is sound practice?

options.text: Submit lowest price regardless of technical compliance
Price before confirming technical solution
Technical compliance first, then price submission with internal walk-away thresholds and risk review
Share price with competitors to stabilise market

explanation: Two-envelope tender strategy balances technical compliance with disciplined commercial pricing.

distractorExplanations.a: Non-compliant technical submissions may disqualify the bid.
distractorExplanations.b: Two-envelope processes separate technical and price evaluation.
distractorExplanations.d: Bid collusion is unethical and potentially illegal.

prompt: At Stage 2, the client asks for a cost plan using elemental format but design is only massing models. What do you do?

options.text: Refuse until full construction drawings exist
Present a trade BOQ as elemental cost plan
Copy another project's cost plan verbatim
Prepare elemental cost plan with assumptions per element, flag uncertainty, and plan measurement at Stage 3

explanation: Early elemental cost plans are assumption-led with clear uncertainty flags.

distractorExplanations.a: Cost plans are required at Stage 2 with stated assumptions.
distractorExplanations.b: Elemental structure follows NRM, not trade breakdown.
distractorExplanations.c: Each project needs tailored assumptions and adjustments.

prompt: A PQS advises on abnormal costs for a constrained city site with party wall awards. How should these appear in the cost plan?

options.text: Separate identifiable allowances for party wall, logistics, and restrictions within relevant elements or risk
Bury abnormal costs in generic preliminaries without identification
Exclude party wall costs as legal not QS
Assume abnormal costs equal 1% always

explanation: Abnormal site costs should be explicitly allowed in cost plans with justification.

distractorExplanations.b: Abnormal site costs should be visible for client decision-making.
distractorExplanations.c: Party wall and access costs are integral project costs.
distractorExplanations.d: Abnormal costs need site-specific assessment.

prompt: Your cost plan reconciliation shows fittings and furnishings underspent but MEP overspent after tender. What advice follows?

options.text: Leave elements unchanged because tender is fixed
Rebalance forecast, confirm scope transfers with design team, and update post-tender cost plan/report
Hide underspend to mask MEP overrun
Cancel MEP works to offset fittings

explanation: Post-tender reconciliation reallocates tender pricing across elements with design confirmation.

distractorExplanations.a: Post-tender cost plans reflect tender pricing and scope allocation.
distractorExplanations.c: Transparent reconciliation supports client budget control.
distractorExplanations.d: Advice must respect design requirements and contract scope.

prompt: A contractor asks the PQS to produce the employer's cost plan and also price the same works commercially. What ethical issue arises?

options.text: No issue if both fees are paid
Commercial pricing improves cost plan accuracy automatically
Conflict of interest — separate teams or declined appointment needed to protect client and market fairness
Share the contractor's margin target with the employer

explanation: Independence in employer cost planning conflicts with contractor commercial pricing roles.

distractorExplanations.a: Dual roles can compromise independent cost advice.
distractorExplanations.b: Independence of employer advice must be preserved.
distractorExplanations.d: Confidential commercial data must not cross conflicted roles.

prompt: Design economics at Level 3 includes advising on specification levels. Client wants 'Category A office' but budget suggests 'shell and core'. What do you do?

options.text: Promise Category A without cost consequence
Downspec silently without client approval
Ignore fit-out allowances in shell and core pricing
Define specification tiers with cost ranges and facilitate client choice against budget and market evidence

explanation: Specification advice maps fit-out levels to cost with client-led decisions.

distractorExplanations.a: Specification advice must be budget-linked.
distractorExplanations.b: Specification changes require client-informed decisions.
distractorExplanations.c: Shell and core vs fitted outcomes need clear cost differentiation.

prompt: A cost plan for a data centre must capture redundancy in MEP. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Model N+1 or 2N redundancy costs explicitly in MEP elements with basis of design stated
Use standard office MEP benchmarks
Hide redundancy in contingency without explanation
Exclude generators as landlord supply always

explanation: Specialist buildings need parameter-driven cost plans, not generic benchmarks alone.

distractorExplanations.b: Data centres have atypical redundancy and services intensity.
distractorExplanations.c: Critical design parameters should be visible in cost plans.
distractorExplanations.d: Scope must confirm inclusion of backup power and cooling.

prompt: Commercial management on a framework requires pricing call-off works without full redesign. What pricing mechanism is typical?

options.text: Open-ended cost reimbursement without caps
Priced schedule of rates or bill of quantities with defined adjustment rules for scope changes
Single lump sum for all future works regardless of scope
Verbal rates agreed on site only

explanation: Framework commercial management relies on documented rates and adjustment rules.

distractorExplanations.a: Framework call-offs need defined pricing mechanisms.
distractorExplanations.c: Undefined lump sums create dispute and margin risk.
distractorExplanations.d: Framework pricing requires documented rate schedules.

prompt: An employer requests that consultant fees be included in the project cost plan. How should you treat them?

options.text: Add 10% to construction cost arbitrarily for fees
Exclude all professional fees from project cost
Separate consultant and construction cost groups with clearly defined inclusion/exclusion boundaries
Include contractor profit inside consultant fees

explanation: Project cost plans commonly separate construction and professional fee cost groups.

distractorExplanations.a: Consultant fees should be itemised or allowance-based with scope.
distractorExplanations.b: Total project cost often includes design and PM fees per client brief.
distractorExplanations.d: Fee groups must not double-count contractor commercial elements.

prompt: Value engineering workshop proposes standardising bathroom pods. What cost planning evidence supports the decision?

options.text: Approve pods because they are modern
Reject pods without reviewing supplier data
Ignore installation crane and access costs
Compare pod capital cost, programme saving, quality control, and logistics against traditional build

explanation: Pod VE decisions need capital, programme, and logistics cost comparison.

distractorExplanations.a: VE decisions require quantified comparative evidence.
distractorExplanations.b: Objective analysis should precede rejection.
distractorExplanations.c: Pod solutions have logistics costs that affect net benefit.

prompt: A cost report must explain a £1.1m increase since last month due to cladding specification change. What documentation is expected?

options.text: Change reference, elemental impact, comparison to allowance, and updated forecast with approval trail
State 'cladding more expensive' only
Omit the increase until tender
Spread increase equally across all elements without cause

explanation: Cost variance reporting ties changes to design decisions with quantified elemental impact.

distractorExplanations.b: Variance reports need quantified traceable analysis.
distractorExplanations.c: Timely reporting of material changes is required.
distractorExplanations.d: Variances should attach to causative elements.

prompt: For APC, you must show commercial management on a £35m civils framework. Which example best demonstrates Level 3?

options.text: Attending a single pricing meeting without decisions
Leading supply chain negotiations, risk pricing, and margin governance on multiple call-offs with measured outcomes
Reading tender documents without contributing
Delegating all commercial decisions to legal

explanation: Level 3 commercial management is shown through leadership in pricing, risk, and supply chain outcomes.

distractorExplanations.a: Level 3 requires leadership and evidenced commercial outcomes.
distractorExplanations.c: Passive involvement does not demonstrate Level 3 competency.
distractorExplanations.d: Commercial management is a QS-led competency.

prompt: A client wants to compare modular vs traditional construction for a student accommodation block. What cost planning deliverable helps?

options.text: Traditional cost plan only
Modular supplier brochure as sole evidence
Option appraisal with capital cost, programme, factory/offsite assumptions, interfaces, and risk allowances
Ignore transport and cranage for modules

explanation: Modular vs traditional appraisals need option-specific costs including logistics and interfaces.

distractorExplanations.a: Option appraisals need parallel option-specific cost models.
distractorExplanations.b: Independent cost planning should verify supplier claims.
distractorExplanations.d: Modular costs include logistics and interface works.

prompt: Your Stage 4 cost plan will inform a JCT Intermediate Building Contract tender. Should VAT be in the cost plan?

options.text: Always include VAT inside every elemental rate without stating treatment

Using only software output without review
Delegating all queries to the architect

explanation: Level 3 quantification is demonstrated through leadership of measurement and costing.

distractorExplanations.a: Level 3 requires personal technical leadership and advice.
distractorExplanations.c: Level 3 includes professional judgment on measurement.
distractorExplanations.d: QS leads quantification and contractual scope clarification.

prompt: A BOQ note states 'all quantities are provisional'. Under JCT SBC, tender comparison is affected because:

options.text: Provisional quantities make tender prices non-comparable always
Contractor must ignore quantities entirely
Tenderers may price differently for remeasurement risk; employer should analyse rate build-ups
Employer cannot award contract with provisional quantities

explanation: Provisional quantities require rate-focused tender analysis and remeasurement planning.

distractorExplanations.a: Comparable rate analysis remains possible with provisional quantities.
distractorExplanations.b: Tenderers still price BOQ items for comparison.
distractorExplanations.d: JCT contracts commonly use provisional quantities with remeasurement.

prompt: Pricing a BOQ item for 'Suspended ceiling — tile and grid' requires:

options.text: Guess a single figure from memory
Use architect's ceiling height as price only
Exclude grid system as supplied by others always
Rate build-up from material, labour, waste, access, and smallworks with measured m² quantity

explanation: BOQ pricing links measured quantities to substantiated rate build-ups.

distractorExplanations.a: Pricing requires structured build-up and quantity linkage.
distractorExplanations.b: Height affects components but pricing needs full build-up.
distractorExplanations.c: Scope must be confirmed; grid is typically included.

prompt: An international BOQ uses FIDIC conditions with method of measurement based on UK practice. Quantification should:

options.text: Follow contract-stated measurement method and coordinate with FIDIC re-measurement clauses
Apply only local QS habit with no contract check
Ignore BOQ because FIDIC is lump sum only always
Use JCT valuation rules in Dubai automatically

explanation: International contracts require adherence to stated measurement and FIDIC valuation rules.

distractorExplanations.b: Contract documents define applicable measurement rules.
distractorExplanations.c: FIDIC forms vary; many use BOQ or re-measurement.
distractorExplanations.d: Applicable law and contract determine measurement and valuation.

prompt: Deductions for voids over 0.5 m² in plasterboard linings per NRM2 mean the QS must:

options.text: Never deduct any openings
Measure gross area and deduct qualifying openings per stated threshold
Deduct all openings including small switch drops always
Measure openings only without wall area

explanation: Applying deduction thresholds correctly is core NRM2 measurement skill.

distractorExplanations.a: NRM2 sets deduction thresholds that must be applied.
distractorExplanations.c: Only openings meeting rules are deducted.
distractorExplanations.d: Linings are measured as areas with rule-based deductions.

prompt: A cost plan elemental quantity check against BOQ shows 8% variance in cladding area. Likely cause?

options.text: Variance always means fraud
Ignore variance because cost plan and BOQ never reconcile
Different measurement boundaries, gross vs net, or BOQ includes features not in cost plan
Delete cladding from both documents

explanation: Cost plan to BOQ reconciliation identifies measurement boundary differences.

distractorExplanations.a: Technical measurement differences commonly cause variances.
distractorExplanations.b: Reconciliation between cost plan and BOQ is standard practice.
distractorExplanations.d: Variance should be investigated, not scope deleted.

prompt: Daywork schedule in BOQ appendices supports:

options.text: Replacing all BOQ measured work
Eliminating need for variation records
Setting contractor profit at 50% always
Valuation of instructed work without prior rates using labour/plant/material daywork percentages

explanation: Daywork schedules provide fallback valuation basis for certain instructed works.

distractorExplanations.a: Dayworks are for contingency/instructed work, not whole project pricing.
distractorExplanations.b: Daywork valuations still require records and instruction.
distractorExplanations.c: Daywork percentages are contractually agreed, not arbitrary.

prompt: Quantifying curtain walling to NRM2 typically measures:

options.text: Area of cladding panels in m² stating system type, performance, and excluded adjacent works
Number of fixing brackets only
Weight of glass only without frame
Linear metres of building height only

explanation: Curtain wall BOQ items use m² with performance and system descriptors.

distractorExplanations.b: Curtain wall is primarily area-measured with system description.
distractorExplanations.c: Measurement covers the curtain wall system per BOQ description.
distractorExplanations.d: Area measurement reflects panelised cladding extent.

prompt: A subcontractor refuses to price BOQ item 3.14 citing ambiguous description. QS action?

options.text: Let one tenderer assume scope unilaterally
Issue clarification/amendment via tender query process with consistent addendum to all tenderers
Delete item 3.14 from contract after award
Tell subcontractor to price highest possible sum

explanation: Pre-tender query resolution ensures fair comparable pricing.

distractorExplanations.a: Ambiguities must be resolved fairly across tenderers.
distractorExplanations.c: Missing scope creates dispute; clarify pre-award.
distractorExplanations.d: Clarification should define scope, not invite inflated pricing.

prompt: Remeasurement contract — interim valuation quantities should be:

options.text: Based on contractor's claimed percentage complete only
Ignored until final account
Measured to date per contract rules with substantiated dimension sheets and audit trail
Copied from tender BOQ without site measurement

explanation: Interim remeasurement valuations rely on measured work-to-date.

distractorExplanations.a: Valuations require measured quantities or agreed progress rules.
distractorExplanations.b: Interim valuations need periodic quantity assessment.
distractorExplanations.d: Remeasurement contracts value actual quantities installed.

prompt: NRM2 'deemed included' items in descriptions mean:

options.text: Contractor may omit deemed items without cost
Employer will pay twice if listed elsewhere
All project works are deemed included in one line
Certain ancillary components are included in the main item rate without separate measurement

explanation: Understanding deemed included scope prevents omission and double counting.

distractorExplanations.a: Deemed included items are part of the measured item scope.
distractorExplanations.b: Deemed included prevents double measurement of minor ancillaries.
distractorExplanations.c: Deemed included applies to defined ancillaries, not whole project.

prompt: A QS uses BIM model quantities for structural steel. Professional checking requires:

options.text: Validate model LOD, exclusions, connections, and NRM2/grouping before accepting quantities
Blind acceptance of model export
Reject BIM entirely on all projects
Add 50% to all model quantities without review

explanation: BIM-assisted quantification still requires LOD and rules-based QS review.

distractorExplanations.b: BIM quantities require QS validation against rules and scope.
distractorExplanations.c: BIM can assist measurement with professional oversight.
distractorExplanations.d: Adjustments should be evidence-based, not blanket.

prompt: Contingency allowances in a cost plan differ from BOQ provisional sums because:

options.text: They are identical in all circumstances
Contingency is employer risk allowance often not in tender BOQ; PS are contract items for defined scope
Provisional sums are never in BOQ
Contingency is always paid to contractor as profit

explanation: Distinguishing contingency from provisional sums is essential costing practice.

distractorExplanations.a: Contingency and provisional sums serve different purposes.
distractorExplanations.c: BOQs commonly include provisional sums for undefined elements.
distractorExplanations.d: Employer contingency may never transfer to contractor.

prompt: When costing demolition works in urban site, quantification should capture:

options.text: Only skip hire days
Exclude asbestos survey allowances
Soft strip, structural demolition, temporary propping, waste disposal, and neighbour protection
Measure new build area only

explanation: Urban demolition BOQs include method, protection, and disposal components.

distractorExplanations.a: Demolition measurement includes multiple risk and method items.
distractorExplanations.b: Hazardous materials surveys and remediation may be required.
distractorExplanations.d: Demolition must reflect existing structures to be removed.

prompt: A tender addendum revises BOQ quantities down 12% on earthworks. Tenderers should:

options.text: Ignore addendum and submit original BOQ
Withdraw without informing client
Increase rates by 12% automatically
Reprice or confirm rates remain valid for adjusted quantities per tender instructions

explanation: BOQ addenda require tenderer review and compliant repricing behaviour.

distractorExplanations.a: Tender addenda are contractually part of tender documents.
distractorExplanations.b: Professional practice addresses addenda through repricing or qualification.
distractorExplanations.c: Rate validity depends on market and instruction, not automatic inverse adjustment.

prompt: Unit rate for 'Concrete in foundations' should clarify:

options.text: Strength class, exposure, pour size, formwork interface, and whether reinforcement is excluded
Nothing — one rate fits all concrete
Only the supplier name
Architect's favourite mix code without specification

explanation: Clear BOQ descriptions enable comparable concrete rate pricing.

distractorExplanations.b: Concrete rates vary materially by specification and scope split.
distractorExplanations.c: Technical specification drives concrete pricing.
distractorExplanations.d: Rates must tie to defined specification and measurement rules.

prompt: Quantification on a design-and-build tender where contractor completes design means:

options.text: Employer always provides firm BOQ for all D&B
Employer documents define performance scope; contractor quantifies completed design in tender
No quantities exist in any D&B tender
Measurement rules are optional

explanation: D&B quantification balances employer requirements with contractor design completion.

distractorExplanations.a: D&B may use employer requirements with contractor design responsibility.
distractorExplanations.c: D&B tenders often include pricing schedules or quantities where defined.
distractorExplanations.d: Defined pricing documents still require consistent quantification.

prompt: Pricing logic for preliminaries time-related charges uses:

options.text: Single lump sum with no programme link
Only percentage of measured works without build-up
Weekly/monthly rates for staff and plant with estimated duration linked to programme
Identical prelims on 6-week and 80-week projects

explanation: Preliminaries pricing links time-related costs to programme duration.

distractorExplanations.a: Time-related prelims depend on programme duration.
distractorExplanations.b: Preliminaries need substantive build-up, not only percentage proxy.
distractorExplanations.d: Duration strongly affects time-related preliminary costs.

prompt: A BOQ for tenant fit-out uses 'item' units for reception joinery. Better quantification is:

options.text: Single item 'joinery' for whole building
No description because item unit suffices
Measure joinery in kg of timber only
Detailed item description with nr count, dimensions, materials, and drawing reference

explanation: Enumerated items require rich descriptions for comparable pricing.

distractorExplanations.a: Joinery items need identifiable descriptions for pricing.
distractorExplanations.b: Item units still require scope-defining descriptions.
distractorExplanations.c: Joinery is typically enumerated with specification detail.

prompt: Cross-check between services BOQ and architectural BOQ should identify:

options.text: Duplications, gaps at interfaces, and coordinated provisional allowances
Only spelling mistakes
Nothing — disciplines never overlap
Client legal clauses

explanation: Multi-discipline BOQ coordination prevents gaps and duplication at interfaces.

distractorExplanations.b: Interface coordination prevents scope gaps and double counting.
distractorExplanations.c: MEP and architectural scopes frequently interface.
distractorExplanations.d: Legal review is separate from BOQ interface coordination.

prompt: Waste allowances in material quantities for costing are:

options.text: Never allowed in professional measurement
Applied per material type and site constraints with stated percentage in build-up
Always 25% on every material
Added only at final account secretly

explanation: Pricing build-ups include material-specific waste allowances.

distractorExplanations.a: Waste factors are standard in pricing build-ups.
distractorExplanations.c: Waste varies by material and installation method.
distractorExplanations.d: Waste should be transparent in pricing assumptions.

prompt: An employer requests BOQ aligned to Uniclass for asset management. Quantification impact:

options.text: Uniclass replaces NRM2 measurement rules entirely
Ignore classification as irrelevant to QS
Structure items with Uniclass codes for lifecycle costing and FM integration
Use Uniclass only for landscaping colours

explanation: Uniclass coding supports structured BOQ data for asset lifecycle management.

distractorExplanations.a: Uniclass supports classification; measurement still follows NRM2.
distractorExplanations.b: Classification aids client asset and cost data management.
distractorExplanations.d: Uniclass is a broader building classification system.

prompt: Subcontract BOQ transfer from main contractor should avoid:

options.text: Any description detail
Including drawing references
Agreeing retention and payment terms
Scope gaps at interfaces and inconsistent measurement rules between main and sub BOQs

explanation: Main-to-sub BOQ transfer requires consistent rules and complete interface scope.

distractorExplanations.a: Detail supports scope clarity; the risk is inconsistency and gaps.
distractorExplanations.b: Drawing references aid scope definition.
distractorExplanations.c: Commercial terms are necessary alongside scope.

prompt: Quantity surveyor checking specialist piling BOQ notes 'working load 800kN'. Must verify:

options.text: Pile type, diameter, depth criteria, testing regime, and ground conditions interface
Only colour of piling rig
Architect's ceiling finish
Number of site inductions

distractorExplanations.b: Technical parameters drive piling cost and method.
distractorExplanations.c: Ceiling finishes are unrelated to piling scope.
distractorExplanations.d: Inductions are preliminaries, not piling technical verification.

prompt: For Level 3 APC, explaining BOQ pricing logic to assessors, you should describe:

options.text: That software produced the price without input
How quantities, market rates, labour/plant, risk, and prelims combined into tender pricing decisions
Only the final tender sum
That pricing is confidential and cannot be discussed

explanation: APC quantification includes articulate explanation of pricing build-up logic.

distractorExplanations.a: Level 3 requires personal pricing reasoning.
distractorExplanations.c: Assessors probe build-up logic and judgment.
distractorExplanations.d: Candidates must articulate pricing approach at interview.

prompt: Measured work for waterproofing tanking to basements should include:

options.text: Only primary membrane area without detailing
Roofing felt in plant rooms only
Surface preparation, membranes, protection boards, and detailing at penetrations per scope
Exclude sumps and pipe penetrations always

distractorExplanations.b: Basement tanking scope must match specification.

prompt: A re-measurement BOQ error overstated structural steel by 200 tonnes at tender. Post-contract effect:

options.text: Contractor keeps payment for 200 tonnes not installed
Tender total remains payable regardless
Project is void ab initio
Interim and final valuations use actual measured quantities — employer benefits from rate × reduced qty

explanation: Remeasurement contracts adjust payment to actual quantities at agreed rates.

distractorExplanations.a: Remeasurement pays installed measured quantities.
distractorExplanations.b: Remeasurement adjusts quantities, not fixed tender total.
distractorExplanations.c: Quantity errors are corrected through remeasurement mechanism.

prompt: Costing temporary works in BOQ is often handled by:

options.text: Separate preliminaries or provisional sums for major temporary works with design responsibility stated
Ignoring temporary works as contractor problem only
Including in every permanent work rate invisibly
Client always designs all temporary works

prompt: Quantifying insulation to external walls, NRM2 expects:

options.text: Insulation omitted as inside brick rate
Area of insulation type and thickness in m² linked to wall build-up
Count bags of insulation only
Measure insulation in litres
